title: Real System Designs
summary: End-to-end architecture walkthroughs: URL shortener, Twitter-like feed, payment system, and notification service.

sections.content:
## How to Approach System Design

A structured framework:
1. **Clarify requirements** — functional (what it does) + non-functional (scale, latency, availability)
2. **Estimate scale** — daily active users, reads/writes per second, data volume
3. **High-level design** — major components, data flow
4. **Deep dives** — DB schema, critical algorithms, bottlenecks
5. **Identify trade-offs** — CAP, consistency vs availability

## System 1: URL Shortener (bit.ly)

**Requirements:**
- Shorten long URLs to 7-char codes
- Redirect short → long in <10ms
- 100M URLs created/day, 10B redirects/day
- Analytics (click counts, geo)

**Key design decisions:**
- 7-char base62 code = 62^7 = 3.5 trillion unique URLs
- **ID generation**: use distributed ID generator (Snowflake) → base62 encode
- **Storage**: write URL mapping once → read many times (perfect for cache)
- **Redirect**: 301 (permanent, browser caches) vs 302 (temporary, every hit counted)

sections.title: URL Shortener Architecture
sections.content:
graph LR
    Client -->|POST /shorten| API[API Server]
    API -->|generate ID| IDGen[Snowflake ID\nGenerator]
    IDGen --> API
    API -->|store mapping| DB[(PostgreSQL\nid → long_url)]
    API -->|cache| Redis[(Redis\nshort → long)]
    API -->|return| Client
    Client2 -->|GET /abc1234| API2[API Server]
    API2 -->|lookup| Redis
    Redis -->|cache HIT| API2
    API2 -->|301 redirect| Client2
    style Redis fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style DB fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff

## System 2: Twitter-like Feed

**Requirements:**
- Users post tweets, follow others
- Home timeline: tweets from people you follow
- 300M users, 100M tweets/day, 500M timeline reads/day

**Two approaches:**

### Fan-out on Write (Push model)
- When user tweets, push to all followers' timeline caches
- ✓ Fast reads (pre-computed)
- ✗ Slow writes for users with millions of followers (celebrity problem)

### Fan-out on Read (Pull model)
- When user requests timeline, query all followed users' tweets and merge
- ✓ Simple writes
- ✗ Slow reads (N queries per timeline load)

**Twitter's hybrid approach:**
- Regular users → fan-out on write (push)
- Celebrities (>X followers) → fan-out on read (pull) + merge at read time

sections.title: Twitter Feed — Hybrid Fan-out
sections.content:
graph TB
    Tweet[User posts tweet]
    Tweet --> Check{Follower count?}
    Check -->|< 10K followers| FanWrite[Fan-out on Write\nPush to followers' caches]
    Check -->|> 10K followers| Store[Store tweet only]
    Read[User loads feed]
    Read --> Merge[Merge:\nPre-computed cache\n+ celebrity tweets pulled]
    FanWrite --> Cache[(Redis\nTimeline Cache)]
    Store --> DB[(Tweet DB)]
    Cache --> Merge
    DB --> Merge
    style FanWrite fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style Merge fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff

## System 3: Payment System

**Requirements:**
- Process payments, transfer money between accounts
- Exactly-once: never charge twice, never miss a payment
- Strong consistency required — money must not be lost

**Key design decisions:**
- Use **ACID transactions** — not eventual consistency
- **Idempotency keys** — clients send unique key per payment; server deduplicates
- **Outbox pattern** — write to DB + outbox table in same transaction; separate worker publishes events
- **Double-entry bookkeeping** — debit one account, credit another atomically

```
Payment table:
| id | from_account | to_account | amount | status | idempotency_key |

Ledger entries (double-entry):
| account_id | amount | type  | payment_id |
| account_A  | -100   | DEBIT | pay_123    |
| account_B  | +100   | CREDIT| pay_123    |
```

sections.title: Payment Flow with Outbox Pattern
sections.content:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant API
    participant DB
    participant Outbox
    participant Queue
    participant Notification
    Client->>API: POST /payments {amount, idempotency_key}
    API->>DB: Check idempotency_key exists?
    DB-->>API: No (new payment)
    API->>DB: BEGIN TRANSACTION
    DB->>DB: Debit account A
    DB->>DB: Credit account B
    DB->>Outbox: INSERT payment_completed event
    DB->>DB: COMMIT
    API-->>Client: 200 {payment_id, status: COMPLETED}
    Outbox->>Queue: Publish event (async)
    Queue->>Notification: Send receipt email

sections.question: Why does Twitter use a hybrid fan-out approach instead of pure fan-out-on-write?
options: Pure fan-out-on-write uses too much storage
Writing to millions of follower caches for celebrity accounts is too slow and expensive
Pure fan-out-on-read is always faster for all users
The hybrid approach avoids the need for a cache entirely
sections.explanation: Fan-out-on-write becomes prohibitively expensive for celebrity accounts with millions of followers — one tweet would trigger millions of cache writes. The hybrid approach uses fan-out-on-write for regular users (fast reads matter) and fan-out-on-read for celebrities (pull at read time + merge), balancing write cost and read performance.
